claim: A common Obsidian maintenance pattern uses the Periodic Notes plugin to layer daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly/yearly review templates that roll up into one another.
quote: My workflow for periodic (weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly) reviews in Obsidian

claim: Dataview queries are widely used inside weekly review notes to surface fleeting notes, open tasks, and orphaned material for the upcoming review pass.
The Find Orphaned Files and Broken Links plugin emits a markdown report listing notes with no backlinks plus links pointing to non-existent files, supporting routine vault hygiene.
quote: Find orphaned files and broken links - Find files, which are nowhere linked, so they are maybe lost in your vault.

claim: The dedicated Broken Links plugin offers folder, file, and consolidated link views to triage internal links that point to missing files, headings, or blocks.
quote: identify and manage internal links within their vault that point to non-existent files, headings, or blocks

claim: The Nuke Orphans plugin trashes orphaned attachments via a single command with a confirmation prompt, automating the destructive step of attachment hygiene.
quote: Obsidian notes plugin that trashes orphaned files and attachments

claim: Andy Matuschak's evergreen-note discipline frames notes as atomic, concept-oriented units that are progressively rewritten and densely linked over time, which is the core refactor practice digital gardeners adopt.
quote: Evergreen notes are written and organized to evolve, contribute, and accumulate over time, across projects.

claim: Maggie Appleton describes growing evergreens through progressive summarisation - re-reading, bolding, splitting, and re-linking notes as the maintenance loop.
quote: as you read and re-read notes, edit and improve them through bolding, reorganizing text, adding links to other notes and external resources, and decomposing notes into smaller ones

claim: Quartz v4 is a free static-site generator that ships Obsidian compatibility, full-text search, graph view, wikilinks, transclusions, backlinks, LaTeX, syntax highlighting, popover previews, and i18n out of the box.
quote: Obsidian compatibility, full-text search, graph view, wikilinks, transclusions, backlinks, Latex, syntax highlighting, popover previews, Docker Support, internationalization, comments and many more

claim: Obsidian Publish costs $8/month annual or $10/month, while Quartz, MkDocs, Hugo/Jekyll/Eleventy and the Enveloppe/Share Note plugins are free, and Flowershow sits at $50/yr; Quartz initial setup runs roughly 30-60 minutes.
quote: $8/month annual, $10/month monthly... Initial setup takes 30-60 minutes for command-line users.

claim: A working Quartz publishing pattern keeps the vault as the source of truth, gates publication with frontmatter publish: true via Quartz's ExplicitPublish filter, and protects attachments by requiring a public- prefix.
quote: ExplicitPublish(), [which] only forwards Markdown pages where the frontmatter has `publish: true` set.

claim: Material for MkDocs positions itself as an extensible open-source documentation theme aimed at making knowledge-sharing easier and more polished, suitable when a vault is more documentation than garden.
quote: Material for MkDocs is described as an extensible, Open Source tool that makes sharing knowledge both easier and beautiful for writers and learners.

claim: Astro Starlight provides a ready-made docs theme on top of Astro that is fast, accessible, and easy to maintain, an attractive option for JS-leaning gardeners migrating from MkDocs.
quote: Astro Starlight is a documentation theme built on the Astro web framework that provides a ready-made structure for creating documentation websites that are fast, accessible, and easy to maintain.

claim: Maggie Appleton's garden remains a canonical public example: visual essays on programming, design and anthropology arranged non-linearly with categories, tags, and curated imagery rather than chronological feed.
quote: filled with visual essays on programming, design, and anthropology... non-linear structure

claim: Joel Hooks' garden is frequently cited as a tone-setting example that explicitly rejects the blog frame in favour of perpetually-edited notes.
quote: My blog is a digital garden, not a blog

claim: The best-of-digital-gardens list maintains a ranked inventory of public second-brain sites that gardeners use as benchmarks for layout, linking density, and update cadence.
quote: Ranked list of awesome digital gardens / second brains
